content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 5 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 202–203 | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_005_page_001.jpg, DEU_005_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 147–148

---

1 And Moshe called to all Yisra'el and said to them: "Hear, O Yisra'el, the statutes and the ordinances that I am speaking in your ears today, and you shall learn them and you shall take heed to do them.

2 The LORD our God cut with us a covenant at Horeb.

3 Not with our fathers did the LORD cut this covenant, but with us — we, these [who are] here today, all of us living.

4 Face to face the LORD spoke with you at the mountain from the midst of the fire —

5 I was standing between the LORD and you at that time to declare to you the word of the LORD — for you were afraid of the fire and did not go up onto the mountain — saying:

6 'I [am] the LORD your God who brought you out from the land of Mitsrayim, from the house of slaves.

7 You shall have no other gods before my face.

8 You shall not make for yourself a carved image — any form that [is] in the heavens above or that [is] on the earth below or that [is] in the waters below the earth.

9 You shall not bow down to them and you shall not serve them, for I [am] the LORD your God — a jealous God [El qanna] — visiting the iniquity of fathers upon sons and upon the third and upon the fourth [generation] of those who hate me,

10 and showing covenant-faithfulness [chesed] to thousands, to those who love me and to those who keep my commandments.

11 You shall not lift up the name of the LORD your God to emptiness [la-shav'], for the LORD will not acquit [the one who] lifts up his name to emptiness.

12 Keep the Shabbat day to sanctify it, as the LORD your God commanded you.

13 Six days you shall labor and do all your work.

14 But the seventh day [is] Shabbat to the LORD your God. You shall not do any work — you, and your son, and your daughter, and your male servant, and your female servant, and your ox, and your donkey, and all your beast, and your sojourner who is within your gates — so that your male servant and your female servant may rest as [you rest].

15 And you shall remember that you were a slave in the land of Mitsrayim, and the LORD your God brought you out from there with a strong hand and with an outstretched arm; therefore the LORD your God commanded you to do the Shabbat day.

16 Honor your father and your mother, as the LORD your God commanded you, so that your days may be lengthened and so that it may go well with you upon the soil that the LORD your God is giving to you.

17 You shall not murder [lo tirtsach].

18 And you shall not commit adultery [lo tin'af].

19 And you shall not steal [lo tignov].

20 And you shall not testify against your neighbor [as] a witness of falsehood [lo-ta'aneh ve-re'akha ed shav'].

21 And you shall not covet your neighbor's wife, and you shall not desire your neighbor's house, his field, and his male servant, and his female servant, his ox, and his donkey, and anything that [belongs to] your neighbor.

22 These words the LORD spoke to all your assembly at the mountain from the midst of the fire, the cloud, and the thick darkness — a great voice and he added not [to it]; and he wrote them upon two tablets of stone and gave them to me.

23 And it was, when you heard the voice from the midst of the darkness, and the mountain was burning with fire, that you drew near to me — all the heads of your tribes and your elders —

24 and you said: "Behold, the LORD our God showed us his glory and his greatness, and his voice we heard from the midst of the fire; this day we have seen that God speaks with man and he lives.

25 And now, why should we die? For this great fire will consume us; if we continue hearing the voice of the LORD our God any more, then we shall die.

26 For who [is there of] all flesh who has heard the voice of the living God speaking from the midst of the fire as we [have] and has lived?

27 You go near and hear all that the LORD our God shall say, and you speak to us all that the LORD our God speaks to you, and we shall hear and do [it]."

28 And the LORD heard the voice of your words when you spoke to me, and the LORD said to me: "I have heard the voice of the words of this people that they have spoken to you. They have done well [in] all that they have spoken.

29 O that there were in their heart such a heart as this, to fear me and to keep all my commandments all the days, so that it might go well with them and with their sons forever!

30 Go, say to them: 'Return to your tents.'

31 But you — stand here with me, and I will speak to you all the commandment and the statutes and the ordinances that you shall teach them, and they shall do [them] in the land that I am giving to them to possess it.'

32 And you shall take heed to do as the LORD your God commanded you; you shall not turn aside to the right or to the left.

33 In all the way that the LORD your God commanded you, you shall walk, so that you may live and [it] may be well with you and you may lengthen days in the land that you shall possess.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**v.2 — cut with us a covenant at Horeb:** *karat imanu berit be-Chorev* — the idiom *karat berit* (to cut a covenant) reflects the ancient treaty-ratification rite of cutting animals (cf. Gen 15). Horeb is the Deuteronomic name for Sinai (the name Sinai appears only once in Deuteronomy, at 33:2). The LXX (p. 147): *διέθετο Κύριος ὁ θεὸς ὑμῶν πρὸς ὑμᾶς διαθήκην* — "disposed/arranged a covenant," a more contractual Greek rendering.

**v.3 — Not with our fathers:** *lo et-avoteinu karat YHWH et-ha-berit ha-zot* — a striking statement. In context, "our fathers" means the Exodus generation who died in the wilderness; "us" is the new generation about to enter. Moshe is emphasizing the immediacy and personal application of the covenant to the current audience. The LXX preserves this without softening.

**v.4 — Face to face:** *panim be-fanim* — literally "face in face." The idiom denotes direct, unmediated encounter. The parallel with Exodus 33:11 ("the LORD spoke to Moshe face to face as a man speaks to his friend") is clear. Yet v.5 immediately qualifies this: Moshe stood as mediator. The tension is unresolved in the text; EMET preserves it.

**v.11 — la-shav' ("to emptiness/vanity"):** The third commandment prohibition. *Shav'* means emptiness, vanity, nothingness, or falsehood. The prohibition covers invoking the divine name in magical formulas, perjury (false oaths), or meaningless/frivolous use. EMET renders *la-shav'* as "to emptiness" rather than "in vain" to preserve the fullness of the term. The LXX: *ἐπὶ ματαίῳ* — upon vanity/emptiness — exact.

**v.12 — as the LORD your God commanded you:** This phrase — *ka'asher tsivvekha YHWH Elohekha* — appears in Deuteronomy's version of the Shabbat commandment but not in the Exodus 20 version. This is one of the key textual differences between the two Decalogue accounts. Similarly, v.15 grounds Shabbat in the Exodus (redemption from slavery) while Exodus 20:11 grounds it in creation. The LXX Vaticanus (p. 147) preserves "as the LORD your God commanded you."

**vv.14–15 — Shabbat rationale — slavery vs. creation:** Deuteronomy 5:15 gives the humanitarian-theological rationale for Shabbat: "You shall remember that you were a slave in Mitsrayim." Exodus 20:11 gives the cosmological rationale: "For in six days the LORD made heaven and earth." The two accounts address different aspects of Shabbat observance — the Deuteronomic account emphasizes social equity (servants rest equally); the Exodus account emphasizes imitatio Dei. EMET does not harmonize — both accounts stand.

**v.14 — so that your male servant and your female servant may rest as [you rest]:** *le-ma'an yanuach avdekha va-amatekha kamokha* — literally "in order that your male-servant and your female-servant may rest like you." The Deuteronomic Decalogue uniquely includes the explicit purpose clause emphasizing the servant's rest. The LXX: *ἵνα ἀναπαύσηται ὁ παῖς σου καὶ ἡ παιδίσκη σου ὥσπερ καί σύ*.

**v.17 — lo tirtsach:** Traditionally "You shall not kill." The Hebrew root *ratsach* is narrower than *harag* (to kill generally) or *mut* (to cause death). It denotes unlawful killing, including murder and possibly manslaughter. EMET renders "murder" to preserve the specificity. The LXX: *οὐ φονεύσεις* — "you shall not murder/slay" — a verb used of unlawful homicide.

**v.18 — lo tin'af:** "You shall not commit adultery." Restricted in the ancient context to sexual relations with another man's wife (not general sexual immorality). The LXX: *οὐ μοιχεύσεις*.

**v.20 — ed shav' ("witness of falsehood/emptiness"):** This version uses *shav'* ("emptiness, falsehood") while Exodus 20:16 uses *shaqer* ("lie"). EMET notes the difference: *shav'* has broader semantic range — it may include technically true but misleading testimony, or testimony offered frivolously. The LXX Vaticanus (p. 147) at this verse: *μάρτυς ψευδής* — "false witness" — harmonizing toward Exodus's *shaqer*.

**v.21 — you shall not covet / you shall not desire:** Two verbs in sequence in Deuteronomy: *lo tachmod* (you shall not covet, desire possessively) for the wife; *lo tit'aveh* (you shall not desire, crave) for the house, field, servants, animals. Exodus 20:17 uses only *lo tachmod* for the entire prohibition, and the order is different (house before wife). Deuteronomy separates the wife into her own clause and uses a second verb for property. The LXX Vaticanus (p. 147–148) follows the Deuteronomic order with *οὐκ ἐπιθυμήσεις* (for wife) and *οὐκ ἐπιθυμήσεις* repeated — using the same verb for both clauses, which does not preserve the Hebrew distinction between *chamad* and *ta'avah*.

**v.22 — a great voice and he added not:** *qol gadol ve-lo yasaf* — literally "a great voice and he did not add." This cryptic phrase means either (a) the LORD spoke once and ceased adding further words, or (b) the voice was incomparably great — "he did not add" meaning no further voice has ever equaled it. The MT is deliberately ambiguous. LXX: *φωνὴ μεγάλη, οὐ προσέθηκεν* — exact.

**v.24 — and he lives:** *va-yichi* — the singular form is notable — "and he [man] lives." The reference is to the continued life of the people despite direct divine speech. The LXX: *καὶ ἔζη* — singular, exact.

**v.29 — O that there were in their heart such a heart as this:** *mi-yitten ve-hayah levavam zeh lahem* — literally "who will give that this heart of theirs were to them." The *mi-yitten* (who will give?) is the Hebrew optative formula — "would that, if only." The divine wish that the people maintain this posture of reverence is poignant. The LXX: *τίς δώσει οὕτως εἶναι τὴν καρδίαν αὐτῶν ἐν αὐτοῖς* — exact.

— EMET
